name: Product Rule
plain: n1 × n2 × ⋯ × nm

name: Sum Rule
plain: n1 + n2 + ⋯ + nm

name: Subtraction Rule (Inclusion–Exclusion, 2 sets)
plain: |A₁ ∪ A₂| = |A₁| + |A₂| − |A₁ ∩ A₂|

name: Division Rule
plain: distinct outcomes = n / d

name: Derangement (no element in its own position)
Pigeonhole Principle
plain: k+1 objects into k boxes ⟹ some box has ≥ 2 objects

name: Generalized Pigeonhole Principle
plain: N objects into k boxes ⟹ some box has ≥ ⌈N/k⌉ objects

name: Minimum N to Guarantee r Objects in One Box
plain: N_min = k(r−1) + 1

name: Symmetry Identity
plain: C(n,r) = C(n, n−r)

name: Number of Subsets of an n-Element Set
plain: Number of subsets = 2ⁿ

name: Total Functions from an m-Element Set to an n-Element Set
plain: nᵐ

name: One-to-One (Injective) Functions from an m-Element Set to an n-Element Set
Number of Even Permutations of n Objects
plain: n!/2   (for n ≥ 2)

name: r-Permutation with Repetition
plain: nʳ

name: Non-Negative Integer Solutions to x₁ + x₂ + ⋯ + xₙ = r
plain: x₁ + x₂ + ⋯ + xₙ = r  ⟹  C(n+r−1, r) non-negative integer solutions

name: Multinomial Coefficient (Permutations with Indistinguishable Objects)
plain: n! / (n₁! · n₂! · ⋯ · nₖ!)  where  n₁ + n₂ + ⋯ + nₖ = n

name: Distributing n Distinguishable Objects into k Distinguishable Boxes
Distributing n Distinguishable Objects into k Indistinguishable Boxes
plain: Σ S(n,j)  for j = 1 to k  [no simple closed form]

name: Distributing r Indistinguishable Objects into n Distinguishable Boxes
plain: C(n+r−1, r)

name: Distributing r Indistinguishable Objects into k Indistinguishable Boxes
plain: pₖ(r) — number of integer partitions of r into at most k parts [no closed form]

name: Binomial Theorem
plain: (x+y)ⁿ = Σ C(n,j) xⁿ⁻ʲ yʲ   (j = 0 to n)

name: Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 2ⁿ
plain: Σ C(n,k) = 2ⁿ  (k = 0 to n)

name: Alternating Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 0
plain: Σ (−1)ᵏ C(n,k) = 0  (k = 0 to n)

name: Weighted Sum of Binomial Coefficients = 3ⁿ
plain: Σ C(n,k) · 2ᵏ = 3ⁿ  (k = 0 to n)

name: Vandermonde's Identity
plain: C(m+n, r) = Σ C(m, r−k)·C(n, k)  (k = 0 to r)

name: Corollary to Vandermonde's Identity
plain: Σ C(n,k)² = C(2n,n)  (k = 0 to n)

name: Hockey Stick Identity (Theorem 4)
plain: Σ C(j,r) = C(n+1, r+1)  (j = r to n)

name: Telescoping Series
plain: Σ (f(x) − f(x+1)) = f(1) − f(m+1)   — middle terms cancel

name: Fermat's Little Theorem
plain: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p)   when p is prime and p does not divide a

name: Series for sum of n·xⁿ
plain: sum n x^n = x/(1-x)^2

name: Coefficient Extraction
plain: [x^n] G(x) = a_n (the coefficient of x^n)

name: Generating Function of the Fibonacci Numbers
Unfolding / Iteration (a_n = a_{n-1} + g(n))
plain: a_n = a_0 + sum_{j=1..n} g(j)

name: Binet's Formula (Fibonacci closed form)
plain: F_n = (phi^n - psi^n)/sqrt(5), phi=(1+sqrt5)/2, psi=(1-sqrt5)/2

name: Lucas Numbers (closed form)
plain: L_n = phi^n + psi^n, phi=(1+sqrt5)/2, psi=(1-sqrt5)/2

name: Fixed Point of a Recurrence (limit)
plain: If x_{n+1} = f(x_n) and x_n -> L, then L = f(L)

name: Sum of the First n Positive Integers
Sum of the First n Squares
Sum of Harmonic Numbers
plain: sum_{j=1..n} H_j = (n+1) H_n - n

name: Edges in a Complete Graph K_n
Number of Labelled Simple Graphs on n Vertices
Number of k-Cycles in a Complete Graph K_n
Handshaking Lemma
plain: sum of deg(v) over all v = 2|E|

name: Even Number of Odd-Degree Vertices
plain: the number of odd-degree vertices is even

name: Edge Bound for Simple Planar Graphs
plain: |E| <= 3|V| - 6 for a simple connected planar graph with |V| >= 3

name: Minimum-Degree Bound from the Degree Sum
plain: delta(G) * |V| <= sum deg(v) = 2|E|

name: Edges in a Tree
plain: a tree on |V| vertices has exactly |V|-1 edges (and is acyclic and connected)

name: Havel-Hakimi Graphical Sequence Test
plain: to test if a non-increasing degree sequence is realisable by a simple graph: remove the largest degree d, subtract 1 from the next d entries, re-sort, and repeat; the sequence is graphical iff this reduces to all zeros without producing a negative value

name: Havel-Hakimi Test for Graphic Sequences
plain: Sort degrees non-increasing; remove the largest degree d_1 and subtract 1 from the next d_1 entries; re-sort and repeat. All zeros => graphic; a negative entry => not graphic.

name: Chromatic Number of a Cycle C_n
plain: A cycle on n vertices needs 2 colours when n is even and 3 colours when n is odd; equivalently chi(C_n) = n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2.

name: Clique Lower Bound on Chromatic Number
plain: The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique; if G contains a K_k then at least k colours are needed.

name: Bipartite Characterisation (no odd cycle)
plain: A graph is bipartite (vertices split into two sides with all edges crossing) exactly when it contains no cycle of odd length.

name: Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph
plain: Any bipartite graph that has at least one edge can be properly coloured with exactly 2 colours.

name: Four Colour Theorem
plain: Every planar graph can be properly vertex-coloured using at most 4 colours.

name: Clique Lower Bound for Chromatic Number
plain: The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique, since every vertex of a clique must get a different colour.

name: Vizing's Theorem (Edge Chromatic Number)
plain: For any simple graph, the minimum number of colours to edge-colour it (the chromatic index) is either the maximum degree or one more than it.

name: Greedy (First-Fit) Colouring Bound
plain: Colouring vertices one by one and always choosing the smallest colour not used by already-coloured neighbours produces a proper colouring using at most Delta+1 colours.

name: Independent Set Lower Bound from Chromatic Number
plain: In any proper colouring with chi colours the n vertices split into chi colour classes (each independent), so the largest one has at least n/chi vertices.

name: Edge Lower Bound from Chromatic Number
plain: A graph with chromatic number k must have at least one edge between every pair of colour classes, so it has at least C(k,2) = k(k-1)/2 edges - as many as the complete graph K_k.

name: Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph
plain: chi(G) = 2 for a bipartite graph that has at least one edge; chi = 1 if it has no edges.

name: Hypercube Q_n: Chromatic Number and Diameter
plain: The n-dimensional hypercube Q_n is bipartite so chi(Q_n) = 2, and its diameter equals n (the maximum Hamming distance).

name: Odd Cycle Forces At Least Three Colours
plain: If a graph contains a cycle of odd length it is not bipartite, so it needs at least 3 colours.

name: Adjacency Matrix (definition)
plain: A[i][j] = 1 if there is an edge from vertex i to vertex j, else 0

name: Maximum Edges in a Graph with n Vertices and k Components
plain: max edges = C(n-k+1, 2) = (n-k)(n-k+1)/2

name: Edges in a Tree
plain: a tree on m vertices has exactly m - 1 edges

name: Edges in a Forest
plain: a forest with n vertices and p tree-components has n - p edges

name: Cycle Rank (Circuit Rank / First Betti Number)
plain: number of independent cycles = E - V + (number of components)

name: Edges in a d-Regular Graph
plain: a d-regular graph on n vertices has nd/2 edges

name: Greedy Maximum Independent Set on a Tree (Leaf Rule)
plain: On a tree, a maximum independent set is found greedily by repeatedly selecting a leaf (degree <= 1), adding it to I when none of its neighbours is already in I, then deleting it; runs in O(n).

name: Articulation Point (Cut Vertex)
plain: A vertex v is an articulation point (cut vertex) if removing v (and its incident edges) increases the number of connected components of the graph.

name: Edge Connectivity Bounded by Minimum Degree
plain: The edge connectivity lambda(G) (min-cut cardinality) is at most the minimum degree delta(G), because the edges at any vertex form a cut isolating it; hence a min-cut of size k forces every vertex degree to be at least k.

name: Components After Removing One Vertex
plain: k - 1 <= components(G - v) <= n - 1

name: Edge Count of a Graph Union (Inclusion-Exclusion on Edges)
plain: |E_1 union E_2| = |E_1| + |E_2| - |E_1 intersect E_2|

name: Gallai Identity (Vertex Cover and Independent Set)
plain: alpha(G) + tau(G) = |V|  (max independent set + min vertex cover = number of vertices)

name: Counting Connected Components by Grouping Vertices
plain: components(G) = number of isolated vertices + number of larger connected pieces

name: Eulerian Circuit Condition
plain: A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree

name: Vertex Degree in the Complement Graph
plain: deg in complement = (n-1) - deg in original

name: Edges in a Forest
plain: a forest with n vertices and k components (trees) has n - k edges

name: Maximum Edges in a Bipartite Graph
plain: a bipartite graph on v vertices has at most floor(v^2 / 4) edges, achieved by the balanced complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} with m + n = v

name: Edges in an m x n King-Move Grid Graph
plain: for an m x n grid where cells are adjacent when both coordinate differences are at most 1 (king moves), the edges are horizontal m(n-1) + vertical n(m-1) + two diagonal families 2(m-1)(n-1)

name: Line Graph Adjacency Rule
plain: in the line graph L(G), each edge of G becomes a vertex, and two such vertices are adjacent exactly when the original edges share an endpoint

name: Bridge-Cycle Criterion
plain: an edge is a bridge (cut edge) if and only if it does not lie on any cycle of the graph

name: Number of Hamiltonian Cycles in a Complete Graph K_n
plain: Number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in K_n = (n-1)!/2

name: Uniqueness Criterion for the Minimum Spanning Tree
plain: A connected weighted graph has a unique MST iff every cut of the graph has a unique minimum-weight edge crossing it; all-distinct edge weights is a sufficient special case.

name: Diameter of the Graph Square G^2
plain: If G^2 joins every pair of vertices within distance 2 of each other in G (adjacency from M OR M^2), then diam(G^2) = ceil(diam(G)/2).

name: Maximum Edges in a Disconnected Simple Graph
plain: A disconnected simple graph on n vertices has at most C(n-1, 2) edges, achieved by a complete graph K_(n-1) plus one isolated vertex.

name: Closed Walks via Trace of a Matrix Power
plain: number of closed walks of length k = trace(A^k) = sum_i (A^k)_{ii}

name: Diagonal of A^2 Gives Vertex Degrees
plain: (A^2)_{ii} = deg(i); (A^2)_{ij} = number of common neighbours of i and j (i != j)

name: Cayley's Formula (Spanning Trees of K_n)
plain: number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2)

name: Spanning-Tree Edge Swap on a Fundamental Cycle
plain: T' = T - e + f  =>  w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e)

name: BFS Shortest Path in an Unweighted Graph
plain: in an unweighted (di)graph, the fewest edges from s to t equals the BFS distance, found by exploring vertices layer by layer

name: Minimum-Weight Connector is a Spanning Tree
plain: with positive edge weights, the least-weight subset of edges connecting all vertices is acyclic and connected, i.e. a (minimum) spanning tree

name: Spanning Trees of a Unicyclic Graph
plain: a connected graph with exactly one cycle has spanning-tree count equal to that cycle's length g; for a simple graph g >= 3

name: Eulerian Graph Condition (Even Degrees)
plain: a connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree

name: Smallest Maximal Independent Set of a Path
plain: the smallest maximal independent set (independent domination number) of a path on n vertices is ceil(n/3)

name: Graph Isomorphism Invariants
plain: isomorphic graphs share the same number of vertices, number of edges, sorted degree sequence, and multiset of cycle lengths; any mismatch proves non-isomorphism

name: Edge Condition for a Self-Complementary Graph
plain: edges(G) = edges(G^c) = (1/2)*C(n,2) = n(n-1)/4

name: Standard Invariants of the Petersen Graph
plain: Petersen graph: 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, girth 5, chromatic number 3, independence number 4, has a Hamiltonian path but no Hamiltonian cycle

name: Number of Perfect Matchings of K_{2m}
plain: number of perfect matchings of K_{2m} = (2m)!/(2^m * m!) = (2m-1)!!

name: Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs
plain: for a connected planar graph drawn in the plane, vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2 (the faces include the unbounded outer face)

name: Kuratowski's Theorem (Planarity Criterion)
plain: a graph is planar if and only if it contains no subgraph that is a subdivision (homeomorphic copy) of K_5 or of K_{3,3}

name: Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs
plain: V - E + F = 2

name: Sum of Face Degrees Equals Twice the Edges
plain: sum over all faces of (edges bounding that face) = 2E

name: Restricted Universal Quantification
plain: forall x ( A(x) -> B(x) )

name: Relation Properties in FOL (serial, reflexive, symmetric, asymmetric, transitive)
plain: serial: forall x exists y R(x,y); irreflexive: forall x ~R(x,x); asymmetric: forall x forall y (R(x,y) -> ~R(y,x)); transitive: forall x forall y forall z (R(x,y) AND R(y,z) -> R(x,z))

name: Universal Quantifier over Disjunction (valid direction)
plain: ((forall x)P(x) OR (forall x)Q(x)) -> (forall x)(P(x) OR Q(x))

name: Existential Quantifier over Conjunction (invalid distribution)
plain: Valid only as (exists x)(P(x) AND Q(x)) -> ((exists x)P(x) AND (exists x)Q(x)); the converse fails

name: Universal Quantifier over Implication (Quantified Modus Ponens)
plain: (forall x)[alpha -> beta] -> ((forall x)[alpha] -> (forall x)[beta])

name: Truth Table of Implication (and Vacuous Truth)
plain: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true

name: Truth Rule of the Biconditional
plain: A <=> B is true exactly when A and B have the same truth value

name: Translating 'Some' with Existential and Conjunction
plain: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))

name: Translating 'All' with Universal and Implication
plain: 'All A have property B' = (forall x)(A(x) -> B(x))

name: Quantifier Negation (De Morgan for Quantifiers)
plain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x);  ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)

name: Universal-Conditional Translation ('All A are B')
plain: forall x (A(x) -> B(x))

name: Direction of 'P if Q' and Negation of Implication
plain: 'P if Q' = Q -> P;  ~(A -> B) = A ^ ~B;  A -> B = ~A v B

name: Collective 'A and B' Maps to OR per Object
plain: 'A and B <things> are D' -> forall x ((A(x) v B(x)) -> D(x))

name: 'Each A has a B' Translation
plain: forall x (A(x) -> exists y (B(y) ^ R(x,y)))

name: Unique Existence (Exactly One) in First Order Logic
plain: exists y [P(y) ^ for all z (z != y -> ~P(z))]

name: Quantifier Negation (De Morgan for Quantifiers)
plain: ~for all x phi = exists x ~phi ; ~exists x phi = for all x ~phi

name: Principle of Mathematical Induction
plain: (P(0) ^ for all x (P(x) => P(x+1))) => for all x P(x)

name: Modus Tollens
plain: from (A -> B) and (not B), conclude (not A)

name: Fallacy of Denying the Antecedent (invalid)
plain: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)

name: Contrapositive Equivalence
plain: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)

name: Hypothetical Syllogism (Chain Rule)
plain: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)

name: Biconditional Truth Value
plain: (P <-> Q) is true iff P and Q have the same truth value; false iff they differ

name: Modus Ponens
plain: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B

name: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases)
plain: from (A or B), and that G follows from A and G follows from B, conclude G

name: Conjunction Elimination (Simplification)
plain: from (A and B), conclude either conjunct (e.g. B)

name: Negation of Quantifiers (De Morgan for Quantifiers)
plain: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)

name: Negated Conjunction as Implication
plain: ~(A and B) = (A -> ~B)

name: Material Implication
plain: P -> Q = ~P or Q

name: Categorical "All A are B"
plain: "All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))

name: Tautology / Valid wff
plain: A formula is a tautology (valid) iff it is true under every interpretation/model.

name: Quantifier Order (forall-exists vs exists-forall)
plain: exists y forall x R implies forall x exists y R, but NOT conversely

name: Existential Distributes over Disjunction
plain: exists x (P or Q) = (exists x P) or (exists x Q)

name: Universal Does NOT Distribute over Disjunction
plain: forall x (P or Q) does NOT equal (forall x P) or (forall x Q)

name: Existential Weakening from a Universal (non-empty domain)
plain: On a non-empty domain, forall x exists y R(x,y) implies exists x exists y R(x,y)

name: Validity of a Well-Formed Formula
plain: F is valid (a tautology) iff value(F, I) = True for every interpretation I

name: Material Implication Law
plain: A -> B == not A or B

name: Contrapositive Law
plain: A -> B == not B -> not A

name: Tautological Implication Forces Its Consequent
plain: If X -> Y is a tautology and X is True under I, then Y is True under I

name: Conjunction Implies Shared-Term Disjunction
plain: (a and b) -> (b or c) is a tautology

name: Universal Instantiation
plain: from for-all x F(x) infer F(a) for any constant a

name: Resolution Rule
plain: from (X or L) and (Y or not L) infer the resolvent (X or Y); the empty clause means inconsistency

name: Disjunctive Syllogism
plain: from P or Q and not P conclude Q

name: Propositional Equivalence Laws (distributive, complement, identity)
plain: Distributive: p ^ (q v r) = (p ^ q) v (p ^ r); Complement: p ^ ~p = F; Identity: p v F = p

name: Principal (Canonical) Conjunctive Normal Form via Maxterms
plain: PCNF = AND of maxterms over all 0-rows; in a 0-row a variable appears plain if its value is 0 and negated if its value is 1

name: Tautology Test (truth-table validity)
plain: A formula is a tautology iff it is true under every assignment; to show it is NOT a tautology, exhibit one row that makes it false

name: Truth Value of an Implication
plain: p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False; True in all other cases

name: Translation of 'Only If'
plain: P only if Q == P -> Q

name: Converse of an Implication
plain: converse(P -> Q) == Q -> P

name: Translation of 'Unless'
plain: P unless Z == ~Z -> P

name: Idempotent Laws
plain: p \/ p == p ; p /\ p == p

name: Truth Condition of Material Implication
plain: An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true. Equivalently X -> Y equals ~X v Y.

name: Logical Equivalence via Truth Tables
plain: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table; a single differing row disproves equivalence.

name: De Morgan's Laws
plain: Negating a conjunction flips it to a disjunction of negations, and vice versa. Pushing a negation inward swaps AND with OR and negates each part.

name: Sum-of-Minterms Truth Vector
plain: A function written as an OR of minterms is true exactly on the rows whose minterms appear in the sum; listing those rows gives the function's truth vector directly.

name: De Morgan's Laws
plain: not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B); not(A or B) = (not A) and (not B)

name: 'Only If' as Implication
plain: 'A only if B' means A -> B (B is necessary for A)

name: 'When' Clause as Implication
plain: 'Y when X' means X -> Y (the 'when' clause is the antecedent)

name: Distributive Law (and over or)
plain: A and (B or C) == (A and B) or (A and C)

name: Self-Implication Tautology
plain: X -> X is always True for any formula X

name: Antecedent-Context Simplification of an Implication
plain: If the antecedent A guarantees Q, then A -> (Q -> R) is equivalent to A -> R

name: Vacuous Truth (False Antecedent)
plain: An implication with a False antecedent is automatically True

name: Validity of a Propositional Argument (Tautology Test)
plain: An argument with premises P_1,...,P_n and conclusion C is valid iff the conditional (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is true under every truth assignment; equivalently, there is no assignment making all premises true and C false.

name: Number of Commutative Binary Operations on an n-Element Set
plain: the number of commutative binary operations on a set of n elements is n^(n(n+1)/2), since the symmetric operation table has n(n+1)/2 freely chosen cells (diagonal plus upper triangle) each filled with one of n values

name: Associativity of a Binary Operation
plain: a binary operation * is associative when regrouping the operands does not change the result, i.e. (a*b)*c equals a*(b*c) for all elements a, b, c

name: Commutative Property of a Binary Operation
plain: a * b = b * a for all a, b

name: Associative Property of a Binary Operation
plain: (a * b) * c = a * (b * c) for all a, b, c

name: Identity Element of a Binary Operation
plain: z is an identity if f(a, z) = a = f(z, a) for every a

name: XOR (Exclusive OR) Operator
plain: p XOR q = 1 when p and q differ, equal to (p + q) mod 2

name: NAND Operator (X' + Y')
plain: X NAND Y = X' + Y' = complement of (X AND Y)

name: Bounded Model Size from Existential Witnesses (Prenex FOL)
plain: A prenex first-order sentence with no function symbols and k essential existential witnesses has a model of size at most k (universals can reuse the witness elements).

name: Divisor Characterization of a Prime
plain: An integer n is prime exactly when its only divisors are 1 and n itself (1 satisfies the clause vacuously).

name: Quantifier Distribution over Connectives (constant W)
plain: When W has no free x: forall distributes over v as forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W; exists distributes over ^ as exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W; and in an implication the antecedent quantifier flips, so forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W.

name: Semantic Implication Between FOL Statements
plain: Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.

name: Exclusive-Or Definition
plain: p XOR q is true exactly when p and q differ; it equals (not p and q) or (p and not q) and is the negation of the biconditional

name: Logical Implication (Entailment)
plain: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true

name: Vacuous Truth of Implication
plain: an implication with a false antecedent is automatically true (false -> anything = true)

name: Countability is Closed Under Subsets and Countable Unions
plain: any subset of a countable set is countable; a countable union of countable sets is countable

name: Cantor: Functions N -> {0,1} (Power Set of N) are Uncountable
plain: the set of functions from N to {0,1} (equivalently the power set of N, or infinite binary strings) is uncountable

name: On a Finite Set, Onto Self-Map Implies One-to-One
plain: for a finite set A, a surjective function f: A -> A is automatically injective (and vice versa), so it is a bijection

name: Image of a Preimage is Contained in the Set
plain: f[ f^{-1}[D] ] subseteq D  (with equality on D intersect range(f))

name: Unique Factorization (Prime-Power / Godel Encoding)
plain: N = prod p_i^{e_i} uniquely encodes the exponent list (e_1, e_2, ...)

name: Onto Composition Forces the Outer Function Onto
plain: if h = f o g is onto then f is onto; range(f o g) subseteq range(f)

name: Double Counting of Element-Set Incidences
plain: sum_i f(i) = sum_j |X_j| = number of (i, j) with i in X_j

name: Number of Boolean Functions of n Variables
plain: number of Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n)

name: Number of Onto (Surjective) Functions
plain: Onto functions from an m-set onto a k-set = sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i C(k,i) (k-i)^m = k! * S(m,k)

name: Preimage Distributes over Intersection
plain: f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T) for every function f

name: Involution (Self-Inverse Function)
plain: f(f(i)) = i means f is its own inverse: a bijection made of 2-cycles and fixed points

name: Function Composition
plain: (g o h)(x) = g(h(x)) — substitute h(x) into g wherever its variable appears

name: Group Axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse)
plain: A set G with operation * is a group if it is closed, associative, has an identity e, and every element has an inverse; a monoid satisfies the first three, a semigroup the first two.

name: Abelian (Commutative) Group Condition
plain: A group is abelian when its operation commutes for every pair of elements.

name: One-Step Subgroup Test
plain: A non-empty subset H of a group G is a subgroup if and only if for all a, b in H, the element a * b^(-1) is also in H.

name: Square of a Product in an Abelian Group
plain: In a group, (a*b)^2 equals a^2 * b^2 exactly when a and b commute; thus this identity holds for all pairs precisely in an abelian group.

name: Probability with Equally Likely Outcomes
plain: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)

name: Bijection Exists If and Only If Equal Cardinality (Finite Sets)
plain: two finite sets admit a bijection exactly when they have the same number of elements; a bijection is also a surjection and an injection

name: Image Size Bounded by Forced-Equality Classes
plain: if functional rules force f to take equal values on linked inputs, the number of distinct values f can take is at most the number of equivalence classes those links create

name: Canonical Bijection from the Quotient by f's Kernel onto its Image
plain: for the relation a~b iff f(a)=f(b), the induced map F([x]) = f(x) is well-defined and one-to-one; if f is onto, F is an onto bijection onto B

name: Surjectivity of a Composition Implies the Outer Map is Surjective
plain: if the composition h = g . f is onto its codomain C, then the outer function g must be onto C, because range(g . f) is always a subset of range(g); the inner map f need not be onto

name: Shannon's Expansion of a Boolean Function
plain: any boolean function can be expanded about a variable x_i as f = x_i' f(...,x_i=0,...) + x_i f(...,x_i=1,...); the literals x_i' and x_i act as selectors that pick the correct cofactor

name: Cancellation Laws in a Group
plain: in any group you may cancel a common factor: left cancellation says a*x = a*y implies x = y, and right cancellation says x*a = y*a implies x = y, because every element has an inverse

name: Hierarchy of Algebraic Structures
plain: the standard algebraic structures form a ladder where each adds one axiom: a semigroup has an associative operation, a monoid adds an identity, a group adds inverses, and an abelian group adds commutativity

name: Self-Inverse Element (Involution)
plain: an element a of a group is its own inverse exactly when a^2 = e; such an element has order 1 (the identity) or order 2; non-self-inverse elements pair up as {a, a^{-1}}, which is the basis of the even-order counting argument

name: Semigroup Associativity
plain: a semigroup is a set with a binary operation * that is associative, meaning regrouping does not change the result; no identity, inverses, or commutativity may be assumed

name: Square-Cancellation in a Semigroup
plain: in a semigroup where the squaring map is injective, two elements with equal squares must be equal; to prove p = q it suffices to show p*p = q*q (using associativity to compute the squares)

name: Cosets Have the Size of the Subgroup
plain: every left coset aH = {a*h : h in H} has exactly as many elements as H, because left-multiplication by a (the map h -> a*h) is a bijection from H onto aH

name: Cosets Partition the Group (Equal or Disjoint)
plain: any two left cosets of a subgroup are either identical or completely disjoint, and together the distinct cosets cover the whole group, so they partition G into blocks

name: Lagrange's Theorem
plain: for a finite group G and subgroup H, the order of H divides the order of G; specifically |G| equals the index [G:H] (number of distinct cosets) times |H|

name: Monoid (Identity but Possibly No Inverses)
plain: a monoid is a set with an associative operation and a two-sided identity element; it becomes a group only if, in addition, every element has an inverse

name: Order of an Element
plain: order(x) = smallest positive integer k with x^k = e

name: Generators of a Cyclic Group
plain: an element generates a finite cyclic group exactly when its order equals the group's order

name: Largest Proper Subgroup Size
plain: the largest possible proper subgroup order is |G| divided by the smallest prime factor of |G|

name: Commuting Involutions Generate the Klein Four-Group
plain: two order-2 elements whose product also has order 2 commute, generating a group of at most 4 elements

name: Equivalence Relation
plain: a relation is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive

name: Complement of an Element in a Bounded Lattice
plain: in a bounded lattice with top I and bottom O, an element x is a complement of a when their join is the top (a OR x = I) and their meet is the bottom (a AND x = O); an element can have zero, one, or several complements

name: Divisibility Lattice (GCD as Meet, LCM as Join)
plain: under the divisibility order (a <= b iff a divides b) the meet of two numbers is their gcd and the join is their lcm; on the set of divisors of a fixed n this is a closed, distributive lattice with bottom 1 and top n

name: Lattice (Join and Meet)
plain: a poset is a lattice if every pair of elements x, y has a unique least upper bound x join y (the join) and a unique greatest lower bound x meet y (the meet); a finite lattice always has a top 1 and a bottom 0

name: Complement in a Bounded Lattice
plain: in a bounded lattice with top 1 and bottom 0, an element y is a complement of x when their join is the top and their meet is the bottom; an element may have zero, one, or several complements

name: Powerset Lattice (2^S, subset)
plain: the set of all subsets of S ordered by inclusion forms a lattice in which the join of two subsets is their union and the meet is their intersection; it is a Boolean lattice with 2^|S| elements, bottom empty set and top S

name: Distributive Lattice Law
plain: a lattice is distributive when meet distributes over join (equivalently join over meet) for all elements; the diamond M3 and the pentagon N5 are the two smallest lattices that violate this, and any non-distributive lattice contains one of them

name: Boolean Algebra (Complemented Distributive Lattice)
plain: a Boolean algebra is a bounded lattice that is both distributive and complemented (every element has a complement, which is then unique); failing distributivity or complementation disqualifies it

name: Distributive Law for Lattices
plain: a lattice is distributive when x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) holds for all x, y, z (equivalently the dual x ^ (y v z) = (x ^ y) v (x ^ z))

name: Product of GCD and LCM
plain: lcm(a, b) * gcd(a, b) = a * b for positive integers a, b

name: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
plain: every integer > 1 factors uniquely into a product of prime powers

name: GCD and LCM via Prime Exponents
plain: per prime, gcd takes the minimum exponent and lcm takes the maximum exponent

name: Surjectivity of a Composition
plain: if f o g is onto then the outer function f is onto, but the inner function g need not be onto or one-to-one

name: Mirsky's Theorem
plain: the minimum number of antichains needed to partition a poset equals the length of its longest chain

name: Partial Order Axioms
plain: a relation is a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive

name: Hasse Diagram Cover Relation
plain: draw an edge between x and y only when x < y with no element strictly between them (a cover)

name: Linear Extensions of a Poset
plain: a total order containing a partial order is a linear extension; counting them means counting orderings consistent with all given relations
